# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

A **2-DOF planar manipulator** consists of two revolute joints in the same plane. We derive its forward kinematics step by step.

### Step 1: Assign DH Parameters

For a 2-link planar arm in the XY-plane:

| Joint $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_1$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $L_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

where $L_1, L_2$ are the link lengths in meters.

### Step 2: Construct Each Transformation Matrix

The general DH matrix is:

$$A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting $\alpha_i = 0$ and $d_i = 0$:

$$A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i & 0 & L_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i & 0 & L_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2a: Textual logic** — Since the arm operates in a plane, the rotation about the Z-axis captures orientation, and translation in X-Y captures position.

### Step 3: Compute $A_1$

$$A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1 \cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1 \sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Compute $A_2$

$$A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 \cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 \sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Multiply $T = A_1 A_2$

Compute the upper-left $2\times 2$ block first:

$$\begin{aligned} T_{11} &= \cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1 \sin\theta_2 = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ T_{12} &= -\cos\theta_1 \sin\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 = -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ T_{21} &= \sin\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 + \cos\theta_1 \sin\theta_2 = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ T_{22} &= -\sin\theta_1 \sin\theta_2 + \cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5a: Textual logic** — We applied the angle-sum identities to simplify products of sines and cosines. This is the standard trigonometric compactness for revolute-revolute chains.

Compute the position vector $(p_x, p_y)$:

$$\begin{aligned} p_x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ p_y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Final Forward Kinematics Result

$$T^2_0 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The end-effector position is simply the **vector sum of the two link vectors** rotated by their respective joint angles. This matches the law of cosines geometry and is verifiable by drawing the arm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sedentary Alert Time with Posture Decay

We want to derive a model where the alert interval $T_{alert}$ shortens as posture degrades.

### Step 1: Define Variables
- $T_{base}$ — base alert interval for perfect posture (s)
- $PDI$ — Posture Deviation Index (degrees)
- $\lambda$ — decay constant (1/deg)

### Step 2: Assume Exponential Decay
The longer the worker sits with bad posture, the faster the next alert should fire. An exponential model captures this:

$$T_{alert} = T_{base} \cdot e^{-\lambda \cdot PDI}$$

### Step 3: Boundary Conditions
- $PDI = 0 \Rightarrow T_{alert} = T_{base} = 1800$ s (30 min)
- $PDI \to \infty \Rightarrow T_{alert} \to 0$ (continuous alert)

### Step 4: Determine $\lambda$ from Calibration Data
Suppose empirical data shows that at $PDI = 30°$, the alert time should be **600 s (10 min)**:

$$600 = 1800 \cdot e^{-30\lambda}$$

$$e^{-30\lambda} = \dfrac{600}{1800} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$-30\lambda = \ln\!\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln 3$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 3}{30} = \dfrac{1.0986}{30} \approx 0.0366 \text{ /deg}$$

### Step 5: Generalized Result

$$\boxed{\,T_{alert}(PDI) = 1800 \cdot e^{-0.0366 \cdot PDI}\,}$$

**Step 5a: Textual logic** — Every additional degree of poor posture multiplies the alert interval by $e^{-0.0366} \approx 0.964$. A $10°$ worsening reduces the interval by roughly **32%**, justifying proactive ergonomic intervention.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Exoskeleton Lift Reduction Ratio

A worker lifts a payload of actual weight $W_{actual}$ (N). With exoskeleton assistance providing an upward force $F_a$ at the load, the **effective perceived weight** is:

$$W_{eff} = W_{actual} - F_a$$

The **Lift Reduction Ratio (LRR)** is defined as:

$$LRR = \dfrac{W_{eff}}{W_{actual}} = 1 - \dfrac{F_a}{W_{actual}}$$

### Step 1: Substitute $F_a$ for a Passive Spring Exoskeleton
The assistive force from a spring compressed by $\Delta x$ is:

$$F_a = k_s \Delta x$$

### Step 2: Express LRR in Spring Terms

$$LRR = 1 - \dfrac{k_s \Delta x}{W_{actual}}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example
Given:
- $W_{actual} = 25 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 = 245.25$ N
- $k_s = 5000$ N/m
- $\Delta x = 0.04$ m (4 cm compression during squat-lift)

$$F_a = 5000 \times 0.04 = 200 \text{ N}$$

$$LRR = 1 - \dfrac{200}{245.25} = 1 - 0.8155 = 0.1845$$

### Step 4: Interpret

$$\boxed{\,W_{eff} = 0.1845 \times 245.25 \approx 45.2 \text{ N (equivalent to 4.6 kg)}\,}$$

**Step 3a: Textual logic** — The worker experiences only **18.45%** of the original load — an **81.5% reduction** in spinal loading. This aligns with reported field studies where passive back exoskeletons reduce L5/S1 disc compression by 40–60%.

---

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Integrated PLSS in a Smart Factory

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    worker[Worker Operator] --> exoInput[Exoskeleton Sensor Layer]
    worker --> chairInput[Intelligent Chair Sensor Layer]
    worker --> manipInput[Manipulator Teach Pendant]

    exoInput --> exoProc[Exo Assistive Controller]
    chairInput --> chairProc[Chair Posture AI]
    manipInput --> manipProc[Motion Planner]

    exoProc --> assistOut[Actuator Torque Command]
    chairProc --> fbOut[Haptic Feedback Alert]
    manipProc --> trajOut[Trajectory Reference]

    assistOut --> cloud[Edge Cloud MES]
    fbOut --> cloud
    trajOut --> cloud

    cloud --> dTwin[Digital Twin Sync]
    dTwin --> erp[ERP Resource Allocator]
    erp --> schedule[Shift Schedule Optimizer]

    cloud --> hmi[Operator HMI Dashboard]
    hmi --> worker
```

**Diagram interpretation:**
- The worker is the **central hub** — every system terminates at the human (Industry 5.0 human-centricity).
- The edge cloud aggregates heterogeneous sensor data (exo + chair + manipulator).
- The digital twin maintains a real-time mirror of worker biomechanics and robot state.

---

## 4.2 Manipulator Kinematic Chain — DH Frame Assignment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    base[Base Frame 0] --> joint1[Revolute Joint 1 - Shoulder]
    joint1 --> link1[Link 1 - L1, alpha1, d1]
    link1 --> joint2[Revolute Joint 2 - Elbow]
    joint2 --> link2[Link 2 - L2, alpha2, d2]
    link2 --> joint3[Revolute Joint 3 - Wrist Roll]
    joint3 --> link3[Link 3 - L3]
    link3 --> ee[End Effector Tool Frame]
```

**Diagram interpretation:** Each node represents one rigid body in the open kinematic chain. The cumulative transformation $T^n_0 = A_1 A_2 A_3$ maps joint coordinates to end-effector pose.

---

## 4.3 Intelligent Chair — Sensor-to-Feedback Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    seat[Seat Pressure Matrix] --> norm[Signal Normalization]
    back[Backrest FSR Array] --> norm
    imu[IMU Inertial Module] --> norm
    occ[Occupancy Capacitive Sensor] --> norm

    norm --> feat[Feature Extraction Engine]
    feat --> cls[Posture Classifier Random Forest]
    cls --> state[State Vector s equals upright slouch forward lean]

    state --> rule[Rule Engine]
    rule --> vib[Buzzer Vibration Motor]
    rule --> led[LED Status Ring]
    rule --> disp[LCD Posture Tip]
    rule --> log[Cloud Event Log]
```

**Diagram interpretation:** Pressure and inertial data are normalized, classified into one of three discrete posture states, and routed to multi-modal feedback actuators. The cloud log enables long-term ergonomic analytics.

---

## 4.4 Exoskeleton — Closed-Loop Assist Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    intent[Operator Intent EMG or IMU] --> est[Intention Estimator]
    est --> ref[Desired Joint Angle Reference]

    ref --> ctrl[Torque Controller PID]
    ctrl --> drive[Motor Driver PWM]
    drive --> act[BLDC Actuator at Hip or Knee]

    act --> body[Human Exoskeleton Coupled Body]
    body --> ftSensor[Force Torque Sensor at Cuff]
    body --> imuFB[Exo IMU Feedback]

    ftSensor --> ctrl
    imuFB --> ctrl

    ctrl --> batt[Battery Management System]
    batt --> safetymod[Safety Watchdog e stop]
    safetymod --> ctrl
```

**Diagram interpretation:** Intent estimation (from EMG or IMU) sets the joint reference. A PID controller drives the BLDC actuator. Cuff force sensors and exoskeleton IMUs close the loop. A safety watchdog can hard-cutoff motor power in unsafe conditions.

---

## 4.5 PLSS Deployment Topology — Shop Floor View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    station1[Assembly Station 1 - Manipulator] --> switch[Industrial Ethernet Switch]
    station2[Assembly Station 2 - Intelligent Chair] --> switch
    station3[Assembly Station 3 - Exo Worker] --> switch
    station4[Logistics Cell - Mobile Manipulator] --> switch
    switch --> gw[5G or WiFi 6 Gateway]
    gw --> cloud[Plant Edge Cloud]
    cloud --> mes[MES Server]
    mes --> dBoard[Factory Director Dashboard]
```

**Diagram interpretation:** Each production station emits structured telemetry over industrial Ethernet. The 5G/Wi-Fi-6 gateway uploads to the plant edge cloud where MES scheduling consumes the data.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a manipulator system. List its principal components with one-line functions. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A manipulator system is a programmable, multi-DOF mechanical arm used in industrial production to perform tasks such as pick-and-place, welding, and assembly.

Principal components:
1. **Base** — anchors the manipulator to the floor/workbench.
2. **Links (rigid members)** — connect successive joints; carry the load.
3. **Joints (revolute/prismatic)** — provide relative motion between links.
4. **End-effector (gripper/tool)** — interacts with the workpiece.
5. **Actuators (servomotors, hydraulic cylinders)** — provide driving torque/force.
6. **Sensors (encoders, F/T sensors, vision)** — provide feedback for closed-loop control.
7. **Controller (PLC/industrial PC)** — executes the motion program.

**(3 marks — full credit if 5 components listed with correct functions.)**

### Q2. Differentiate between a passive exoskeleton and an active exoskeleton with two examples each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Passive Exoskeleton | Active Exoskeleton |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | None (uses springs, dampers) | External (motors, batteries) |
| **Energy Storage** | Mechanical (springs, elastomers) | Electrical (Li-ion battery) |
| **Assistive Force** | Constant or position-dependent | Controllable via control loop |
| **Weight** | Lighter (2–6 kg) | Heavier (10–30 kg) |
| **Examples** | SuitX ShoulderX, Levitate Airframe | Sarcos Guardian XO, Hyundai Chairless |

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the kinematic structure of a 6-DOF industrial manipulator with a labeled diagram. Derive the forward kinematics equation using the DH convention for any 2-link sub-chain and explain the Jacobian's role in velocity mapping. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Kinematic Structure (2 marks)**

A 6-DOF manipulator has six revolute joints grouped into:
- **Arm (3 DOF):** Joint 1 (waist/rotation), Joint 2 (shoulder), Joint 3 (elbow) — positions the wrist.
- **Wrist (3 DOF):** Joints 4, 5, 6 — orient the end-effector (roll, pitch, yaw).

The DH parameters $(a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i)$ for each joint are tabulated.

**Step 2: Forward Kinematics of 2-Link Sub-Chain (3 marks)**

Already derived in Section 3.1. Final equations:

$$p_x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

$$p_y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

$$p_z = d_1 + d_2$$

**Step 3: Jacobian's Role in Velocity Mapping (2 marks)**

The Jacobian $J(q)$ is a $6 \times n$ matrix (for an $n$-DOF arm) that maps joint velocities to Cartesian velocities:

$$\dot{\vec{x}} = J(q) \dot{q}$$

For force mapping (virtual work principle):

$$\tau = J^T(q) \vec{F}$$

The Jacobian is essential for:
- **Singularity detection** — when $\det(J) = 0$, the manipulator loses a DOF.
- **Trajectory planning** — converting Cartesian paths to joint trajectories.
- **Force control** — converting measured external forces to required joint torques.

---

#### (b) Discuss the role of intelligent chairs in Industry 5.0 production lines. With a labeled block diagram, explain the sensor-to-feedback pipeline and derive the sedentary alert time formula with boundary conditions. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Role of Intelligent Chairs (2 marks)**

In Industry 5.0, the human worker is treated as the most valuable, irreplaceable asset. Intelligent chairs:
- Prevent Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders (WRMSDs).
- Provide real-time biofeedback to maintain neutral posture.
- Generate longitudinal ergonomic data for management dashboards.
- Enable sustainable productivity by reducing fatigue-induced errors.

**Step 2: Sensor-to-Feedback Pipeline (3 marks)**

Refer to the **Intelligent Chair Block Diagram** (Section 4.3). Pipeline:

1. **Sensor Layer:** Seat pressure matrix (FSR), backrest FSR array, IMU, occupancy capacitive sensor.
2. **Signal Normalization:** ADC conversion, scaling, noise filtering (e.g., moving average).
3. **Feature Extraction:** Center of pressure (CoP), tilt angles, contact area.
4. **Posture Classification:** Random Forest / SVM classifier labels state vector $\vec{s}$.
5. **Feedback Actuation:** Vibration motor, LED ring, LCD display, cloud event log.

**Step 3: Sedentary Alert Time Derivation (2 marks)**

As derived in Section 3.2:

$$T_{alert}(PDI) = T_{base} \cdot e^{-\lambda \cdot PDI}$$

With $T_{base} = 1800$ s and $\lambda \approx 0.0366$ /deg:

| $PDI$ | $T_{alert}$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $0°$ | $30.0$ min | Perfect posture, relaxed interval |
| $15°$ | $22.0$ min | Mild slouch, alert sooner |
| $30°$ | $10.0$ min | Significant slouch |
| $45°$ | $4.6$ min | Critical posture, frequent prompts |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

#### (a) What is an exoskeleton? Classify exoskeletons by body region and power source. Derive the lift reduction ratio for a passive spring exoskeleton and compute the effective payload weight for a numerical case. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**

An exoskeleton is a wearable external structural device that conforms to the human body and provides assistive forces/torques at joints to augment worker capability.

**Step 2: Classification (2 marks)**

By **body region**:
- **Lower-limb** (hip, knee, ankle) — e.g., Ekso GT
- **Upper-limb** (shoulder, elbow) — e.g., SuitX ShoulderX
- **Full-body** (spine + limbs) — e.g., Sarcos Guardian XO
- **Chairless / leg-only** — e.g., Hyundai Chairless

By **power source**:
- **Passive** — springs, elastomers (no electricity)
- **Active** — electric motors / hydraulics (powered)
- **Semi-active** — controllable dampers (MR fluids)

**Step 3: LRR Derivation (2 marks)** — Already shown in Section 3.3.

**Step 4: Numerical Case (2 marks)**

Given: $W_{actual} = 25$ kg, $k_s = 5000$ N/m, $\Delta x = 0.04$ m.

$$F_a = 5000 \times 0.04 = 200 \text{ N}$$

$$W_{actual} = 25 \times 9.81 = 245.25 \text{ N}$$

$$W_{eff} = 245.25 - 200 = 45.25 \text{ N} \approx 4.6 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{LRR = \dfrac{45.25}{245.25} \approx 0.184 \Rightarrow 81.6\% \text{ load reduction}}$$

---

#### (b) Compare and contrast manipulator systems, intelligent chairs, and exoskeletons along the dimensions: (i) purpose, (ii) power source, (iii) human-interaction mode, (iv) typical Industry 5.0 use case. Justify with one industrial example for each. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Comparative Table (5 marks):**

| Dimension | Manipulator System | Intelligent Chair | Exoskeleton |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Purpose** | Automate or assist repetitive heavy/precise tasks | Maintain worker posture and prevent WRMSDs | Augment human strength/endurance |
| **(ii) Power Source** | Electric servomotors / hydraulics | Mains electricity / battery (sensors) | Springs (passive) or batteries (active) |
| **(iii) Human-Interaction Mode** | Collaboration (cobot) or replacement | Direct contact (seated) | Direct wear (full-body contact) |
| **(iv) Industrial Use Case** | Welding on automotive body | Stationary assembly inspection | Overhead pipe installation in shipyards |
| **Industrial Example** | KUKA KR 16 in BMW welding cell | BMW Spartanburg ergonomic chair + IMU system | Sarcos Guardian XO at Ford assembly plant |

**Justification (2 marks):**
- **Manipulator:** KUKA KR 16 R1610 provides 16 kg payload, 1610 mm reach — ideal for spot welding car bodies with $\pm 0.04$ mm repeatability.
- **Intelligent chair:** Integrated with operator's wearable IMU, alerts reduce lumbar injuries by **38%** in long-cycle assembly stations.
- **Exoskeleton:** Sarcos Guardian XO provides 30:1 lift augmentation, enabling 8-hour shifts without fatigue in tire-lift operations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating prosthetic with exoskeleton** — prosthetic *replaces* a missing limb; exoskeleton *augments* an existing limb. (-1 mark if confused)
> 2. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — LRR is dimensionless; $W_{eff}$ must carry units of N or kg. (-0.5 mark per missing unit)
> 3. **Forgetting the Jacobian's dual role** — students often mention only velocity mapping, missing the force-mapping $\tau = J^T F$ principle. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Writing "robot" instead of "manipulator"** — the KTU 2024 scheme is precise; a manipulator is a subset of robots (the mechanical arm), not the whole system including controller.
> 5. **Skipping the angle-sum identity step** — when multiplying DH matrices, the trigonometric simplification $\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ must be shown explicitly.
> 6. **Not specifying active vs passive** — when discussing exoskeletons, always declare which type before quoting assistive torque values.

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Manipulator System:** Programmable multi-DOF mechanical arm; key metric = **DOF (typically 6)**, **repeatability ($\pm 0.02$ mm)**, **payload**.
- [x] **Forward Kinematics:** Uses **DH parameters** $(a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i)$; produces homogeneous transform $T^n_0$.
- [x] **Jacobian $J(q)$:** Maps joint space → Cartesian space ($\dot{\vec{x}} = J\dot{q}$) AND maps force → torque ($\tau = J^T \vec{F}$).
- [x] **Manipulability index** $\nu = \sqrt{\det(JJ^T)}$ — zero at singularities.
- [x] **Intelligent Chair:** IoT-enabled ergonomic seating with **pressure matrix, IMU, FSR sensors**.
- [x] **Key Chair Metrics:** SPI $\leq 35$ kPa, PDI $\leq 15°$, $T_{alert} = T_{base} e^{-\lambda \cdot PDI}$.
- [x] **Exoskeleton:** Wearable augmentation device — **NOT** a prosthetic.
- [x] **Passive vs Active:** Passive = springs; Active = motors + batteries.
- [x] **Lift Reduction Ratio:** $LRR = 1 - F_a / W_{actual}$; typical reduction 60–85%.
- [x] **Industry 5.0 Principle:** All three systems serve the **human-centric** paradigm, augmenting (not replacing) the worker.
- [x] **Industrial Examples:** KUKA KR 16 (manipulator); BMW ergonomic chair + IMU; Sarcos Guardian XO (exoskeleton).
- [x] **Formula Acronym — "SPEAR":** Sensors, Physical structure, End-effector, Actuators, Recognition.
